quiz_questions.question_text: What produces magnetic fields?

options.text: Moving charges (currents) and permanent magnets
Stationary electric charges and neutral conductors
Longitudinal sound waves traveling through metals
Gravitational attraction between massive bodies

quiz_questions.explanation: Magnetic fields are produced by moving charges (currents) and permanent magnets.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI unit of magnetic field?

options.text: Weber (Wb)
Newton (N)
Tesla (T)
Henry (H)

quiz_questions.explanation: The SI unit of magnetic field strength is the Tesla (T).

quiz_questions.question_text: Does a stationary charge experience a magnetic force?

options.text: Yes, always
No, never
In strong fields
Near magnets

quiz_questions.explanation: A stationary charge does not experience a magnetic force. The charge must be moving (F = qvB sin θ).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the force on a moving charge in a B field?

options.text: F = qvB cos θ
F = qv/B
F = qvB sin θ
F = qB/v

quiz_questions.explanation: F = qvB sin θ, where θ is the angle between v and B.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does the magnetic force do work on a moving charge?

options.text: Yes
Only at high speeds
No — it changes direction only
Only for electrons

quiz_questions.explanation: The magnetic force is always perpendicular to velocity, so it does no work — it only changes the direction of motion, not the speed.

quiz_questions.question_text: The magnetic force is perpendicular to:

options.text: Neither
Both v and B
Only v
Only B

quiz_questions.explanation: F = qv × B. The cross product gives a force perpendicular to both v and B.

quiz_questions.question_text: Magnetic field lines outside a magnet go from:

options.text: They don't have direction
East to West
South to North
North to South

quiz_questions.explanation: Outside a magnet, field lines go from the north pole to the south pole. Inside the magnet, they go south to north, forming closed loops.

quiz_questions.question_text: What rule determines the direction of the magnetic force on a positive charge?

options.text: The left-hand rule for positive charges
The right-hand rule for positive charges
The principle of superposition for forces
Lenz's Law for induced current direction

quiz_questions.explanation: The right-hand rule: point fingers in the direction of velocity, curl them toward B, and the thumb points in the direction of the force on a positive charge.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the force on a current-carrying wire in a magnetic field?

options.text: F = BIL sin θ
F = BIL cos θ
F = BI/L sin θ
F = B/(IL) sin θ

quiz_questions.explanation: F = BIL sin θ, where B is field strength, I is current, L is wire length, and θ is the angle between the wire and field.

quiz_questions.question_text: What path does a charged particle follow in a uniform magnetic field?

options.text: A straight line path in the field direction
A circular or helical path perpendicular to B
A parabolic path like a projectile in gravity
An elliptical path around the field source

quiz_questions.explanation: The magnetic force is always perpendicular to velocity, acting as a centripetal force. This causes the particle to move in a circular (or helical if there is a velocity component along B) path.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'magnetic field'?

options.text: A region where a moving charge or magnetic material experiences a force
A region where stationary electric charges experience a repulsive force
A field created only by permanent magnets and not by electric currents
The force between two gravitationally attracted massive objects in space

quiz_questions.explanation: A magnetic field is a region of space where moving charges and magnetic materials experience a force. It is produced by moving charges and permanent magnets.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the 'Tesla' (T) measure?

options.text: The strength of an electric field in volts per meter
The strength of a magnetic field in a region of space
The total magnetic flux through a closed surface area
The inductance of a coil measured in henries per meter

quiz_questions.explanation: The Tesla (T) is the SI unit of magnetic field strength. 1 T = 1 kg/(A·s²). The Earth's magnetic field is about 5 × 10⁻⁵ T.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'right-hand rule' in magnetism?

options.text: A rule for determining the direction of force on a positive moving charge in a field
A rule that states magnetic force is always directed toward the right side
A rule for calculating the magnitude of the magnetic force on a charge
A rule that only applies to permanent magnets and not to electromagnets

quiz_questions.explanation: The right-hand rule determines the direction of the magnetic force: point fingers along v, curl toward B, and the thumb gives the force direction on a positive charge.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'magnetic field line'?

options.text: A physical wire that creates a magnetic field when current passes through
An imaginary line showing the direction a north pole would move in the field
The boundary between a magnetic north region and south region in space
A line along which the magnetic force has its maximum possible magnitude

quiz_questions.explanation: Magnetic field lines are imaginary lines that show the direction of the magnetic field at every point. A compass needle (north pole) aligns along these lines.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'solenoid'?

options.text: A single straight wire carrying a large electrical current through a circuit
A coil of wire that produces a uniform magnetic field inside when current flows
A permanent magnet shaped like a horseshoe for attracting iron objects
A device that measures the strength of a magnetic field at a point

quiz_questions.explanation: A solenoid is a tightly wound coil of wire. When current flows through it, it produces a nearly uniform magnetic field inside, similar to a bar magnet.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an 'electromagnet'?

options.text: A permanent magnet that never loses its magnetic field strength over time
A magnet created by running electric current through a coil of wire
A natural mineral that has inherent magnetic properties from formation
A device that converts magnetic energy directly into electrical energy

quiz_questions.explanation: An electromagnet is a magnet produced by electric current flowing through a coil. Its strength can be controlled by changing the current, and it can be turned on and off.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'Lorentz force' refer to?

options.text: The total force on a charged particle from both electric and magnetic fields
The gravitational force acting on a charged particle near a massive body
The force between two permanent magnets placed near each other in space
The friction force that opposes the motion of charges in a conductor wire

quiz_questions.explanation: The Lorentz force is the total electromagnetic force on a charged particle: F = qE + qv × B. It combines both the electric force and the magnetic force.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'ferromagnetic' mean?

options.text: A material that is repelled by all magnetic fields under every condition
A material that can be strongly magnetized, like iron, nickel, or cobalt
A material that is completely unaffected by any magnetic field at all
A material that conducts electricity but cannot be magnetized in any way

quiz_questions.explanation: Ferromagnetic materials (iron, nickel, cobalt) can be strongly magnetized. Their atomic magnetic domains can align, creating a strong net magnetic field.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'magnetic flux density'?

options.text: Another name for the magnetic field strength B measured in teslas
The total magnetic flux passing through a surface measured in webers
The number of magnetic field lines drawn in a diagram per unit length
The rate of change of magnetic flux that induces an electromotive force

quiz_questions.explanation: Magnetic flux density (B) is another term for the magnetic field strength, measured in teslas (T). It represents the amount of magnetic flux per unit area.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are 'magnetic poles'?

options.text: The regions at each end of a magnet where the magnetic field is strongest
The center of a magnet where the magnetic field is at its maximum value
Points in space where the magnetic field is exactly equal to zero always
The electric charges that create the magnetic field around a wire conductor

quiz_questions.explanation: Magnetic poles are the two ends of a magnet (north and south) where the magnetic field is strongest. Like poles repel; unlike poles attract. Magnetic monopoles have never been observed.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student moves a positive charge to the right in a magnetic field pointing into the page. Using the right-hand rule, which direction is the force?

options.text: The force on the charge is directed to the right
The force on the charge is directed downward
The force on the charge is directed upward
The force on the charge is directed to the left

quiz_questions.explanation: Right-hand rule: fingers point right (v), curl into the page (B), thumb points upward. The force on the positive charge is upward.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student places a compass near a straight wire carrying current upward. Which way does the compass needle point?

options.text: The needle points in the same direction as the current (upward)
The needle aligns tangent to a circle around the wire per the right-hand rule
The needle points directly toward the wire from any position near it
The needle spins randomly because wires do not create magnetic fields

quiz_questions.explanation: A current-carrying wire creates circular magnetic field lines around it. Using the right-hand rule (thumb along current, fingers curl in the field direction), the compass needle aligns tangent to these circles.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student shoots an electron straight into a uniform magnetic field. The electron curves into a circle. Why doesn't the electron speed up or slow down?

options.text: The magnetic field is too weak to change the electron's speed at all
The magnetic force is always perpendicular to velocity so it does no work
The electron has too much mass for the magnetic force to accelerate it
The electric field inside the magnet cancels out the magnetic acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: The magnetic force F = qv × B is always perpendicular to the velocity, so it can only change the direction of motion, not the speed. No work is done, so kinetic energy (and speed) remain constant.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, a student passes current through a wire placed between two magnets. The wire jumps sideways. What principle explains this?

options.text: Electromagnetic induction generates a voltage that repels the nearby magnets
A current-carrying wire in a magnetic field experiences a force (F = BIL)
The wire becomes magnetized and repels both of the magnets equally
Thermal expansion from the current causes the wire to bend sideways

quiz_questions.explanation: A current-carrying wire in an external magnetic field experiences a force F = BIL sin θ. This is the motor effect and is the principle behind electric motors.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student increases the current in a solenoid. What happens to the magnetic field inside?

options.text: The magnetic field inside the solenoid decreases in strength
The magnetic field inside the solenoid increases in strength
The magnetic field stays exactly the same regardless of current
The magnetic field reverses direction inside the solenoid coil

quiz_questions.explanation: The magnetic field inside a solenoid is B = μ₀nI, directly proportional to current I. Increasing current increases the field strength.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student wraps an iron nail with wire and connects it to a battery. The nail picks up paper clips. When the battery is disconnected, the clips fall. Why?

options.text: The nail was permanently magnetized but the battery absorbed the magnetism
The nail was a temporary electromagnet that loses its field without current
The paper clips became too heavy for the nail to hold without power
The wire melted from the battery heat and released the paper clips

quiz_questions.explanation: The current-carrying coil around the iron nail created an electromagnet. Iron is a soft magnetic material that loses most of its magnetism when the current stops, so the clips fall off.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student learns that electric motors use the force on a current-carrying coil in a magnetic field. What converts the electrical energy?

options.text: The battery converts electrical energy into kinetic energy of rotation
The magnetic force on the current-carrying coil converts it into rotation
Friction between the coil and magnets generates the rotational motion
Heat from the current causes expansion that pushes the coil around

quiz_questions.explanation: In a motor, current flows through a coil placed in a magnetic field. The force F = BIL on each side of the coil creates a torque that causes rotation, converting electrical energy to mechanical energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student moves a charge parallel to a magnetic field. What magnetic force does it experience?

options.text: Maximum force because the charge is aligned with the field direction
Zero force because sin(0°) = 0 in the force equation F = qvB sin θ
Half the maximum force because the angle is between 0° and 90°
Infinite force because parallel motion maximizes the cross product

quiz_questions.explanation: F = qvB sin θ. When the charge moves parallel to B, θ = 0° and sin(0°) = 0, so F = 0. The charge must have a velocity component perpendicular to B to experience a magnetic force.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student uses a magnetic field to separate charged particles by mass in a mass spectrometer. How does this work?

options.text: Heavier particles are attracted more strongly to the magnetic field source
Different masses curve in different radius circles because r = mv/(qB)
The magnetic field slows down heavier particles more than lighter ones
Lighter particles are absorbed by the magnetic field while heavier pass through

quiz_questions.explanation: In a mass spectrometer, charged particles enter a magnetic field and curve. The radius of curvature r = mv/(qB) depends on mass. Heavier particles follow larger circles, allowing separation by mass.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student places two parallel wires carrying current in the same direction near each other. What happens?

options.text: The wires repel each other and move apart from one another
The wires attract each other and move closer together
Nothing happens because wires do not interact magnetically at all
The wires twist around each other into a spiral shape formation

quiz_questions.explanation: Parallel currents in the same direction attract each other. Each wire's magnetic field exerts a force on the other wire's current, pulling them together. Opposite currents repel.
